original_abstract: The debate around the use of GPT-3.5 has been a popular topic among academics since the release of ChatGPT. Whilst some have argued for the advantages of GPT-3.5 in enhancing academic writing, others have raised concerns such as plagiarism, the spread of false information, and ecological issues. The need for finding ways to use GPT-3.5 models transparently has been voiced, and suggestions have been made on social media as to how to use GPT-3.5 models in a smart way. Nevertheless, to date, there is a lack of literature which clearly outlines how to use GPT-3.5 models in academic writing, how effective they are, and how to use them transparently. To address this, I conducted a personal experience experiment with GPT-3.5, specifically by using text-davinci-003 model of OpenAI, for writing this article. I identified five ways of using GPT-3.5: Chunk Stylist, Bullet-to-Paragraph, Talk Textualizer, Research Buddy, and Polisher. I reflected on their efficacy and commented on their potential impact on writing ethics. Additionally, I provided a comprehensive document which shows the prompts I used, results I got from GPT-3.5, the final edits and visually compares those by showing the differences in percentage.
humanized_abstract: There has been quite a bit of discussion in the academic community regarding the use of GPT-3.5 with respect to ChatGPT. While some have discussed the potential benefits for academic writing, others have commented on the potential for plagiarism, the spread of misinformation, and environmental factors. There has been a discussion of the need to “fair use” GPT-3.5 models, and many suggestions regarding how to do so have been discussed on social media. However, no research has been published that systematically evaluates the use of GPT-3.5 models in academic writing, nor the effectiveness of GPT-3.5 models and the “fair use” of them. In this article, I reflect on my personal experience experiment of the use of GPT-3.5 models in academic writing by applying the text-davinci-003 model of OpenAI (GPT-3.5) to assistive tools for writing this article, and evaluating the functionality and effectiveness of five applications modes (i.e., Chunk Stylist, Bullet-to-Paragraph, Talk Textualizer, Research Buddy, and Polisher) and discuss the implications of the use of such models for writing ethics. Appendix contains the full set of prompts, GPT-3.5-generated outputs, final versions of each sample with visual representations of the GPT-3.5 outputs applied to the samples with the differing levels of explanations.
